Okay, I need to figure out the total distance Karl drove. Let me start by breaking down the problem step by step-by-step:
1. Identify key details: Marc's car uses python_exec
Okay, let's see. Karl's car uses 1 gallon every 30 miles. So his fuel efficiency is 30 miles per gallon. His gas tank holds 16 gallons when full. He started with a full tank, drove 420 miles, then bought 10 gallons, and continued driving until his tank was three-quarters full. We need to find the total distance he drove that day.

First, let's break down the problem into parts. The total distance will be the initial 420 miles plus the distance he drove after refueling.

Starting with a full tank: 16 gallons. He drives 420 miles. How much gas does he use for that? Since it's 1 gallon per 30 miles, 420 miles would use 420 / 30 = 14 gallons. So after driving 420 miles, he has 16 - 14 = 2 gallons left.

Then he buys 10 gallons. So his tank now has 2 + 10 = 12 gallons. Now he continues driving until the tank is three-quarters full. Three-quarters of 16 gallons is (3/4)*16 = 12 gallons. Wait, that's interesting. So after refueling, he has 12 gallons, and he drives until it's 12 gallons again? That doesn't make sense. Wait, maybe I made a mistake here.

Wait, three-quarters full of the tank. The tank's capacity is 16 gallons. Three-quarters of that is 12 gallons. So he starts with 12 gallons after refueling, and he drives until the tank is 12 gallons. That would mean he didn't use any gas? That can't be right. There must be a mistake here.

Wait, no. Let me check again. He starts with a full tank (16 gallons), drives 420 miles, uses 14 gallons, so 2 left. Then he buys 10 gallons, so 2 + 10 = 12 gallons. Then he drives until the tank is three-quarters full. Three-quarters of 16 is 12. So he drives until the tank is 12 gallons. But he already has 12 gallons. So he doesn't drive any more? That can't be. That would mean the total distance is 420 miles. But that seems unlikely. Maybe I misunderstood the problem.

Wait, maybe the three-quarters full is after the refueling. Let me re-read the problem. "continued driving until his gas tank was three-quarters full." So after buying 10 gallons, he drives until the tank is three-quarters full. So the three-quarters is relative to the tank's capacity. So 3/4 * 16 = 12 gallons. So he starts with 12 gallons after refueling, and he drives until the tank is 12 gallons. That would mean he didn't use any gas, which is impossible. Therefore, there must be a mistake in my calculation.

Wait, maybe I miscalculated the gas after refueling. Let me check again. He starts with 16 gallons. Drives 420 miles. 420 / 30 = 14 gallons used. 16 - 14 = 2 gallons left. Then he buys 10 gallons, so 2 + 10 = 12 gallons. Then he drives until the tank is three-quarters full. Three-quarters of 16 is 12. So he needs to have 12 gallons left. But he already has 12 gallons. So he can't drive any more. That would mean the total distance is 420 miles. But that seems odd. Maybe the problem is that three-quarters full is after the refueling, but perhaps the three-quarters is of the current tank? No, the tank's capacity is 16 gallons.    be a valid Python snippet that PRINTS the values you want to see back —
